ECONOMICS - COMPONENT 2

© WJEC CBAC Ltd.

 About this marking scheme
 The purpose of this marking scheme is to provide teachers, learners, and other interested parties, with an
 understanding of the assessment criteria used to assess this specific assessment.
 This marking scheme reflects the criteria by which this assessment would be marked in a live series and
 has been prepared for predicted-paper use. It may not be possible, or appropriate, to capture every
 variation that a candidate may present in their responses within this marking scheme. Examiners are
 expected to use their professional judgement to credit alternative valid responses as instructed by the
 document.
 Without the benefit of participation in an examiners' conference, teachers, learners and other users, may
 have different views on certain matters of detail or interpretation. Therefore, it is strongly recommended
 that this marking scheme is used alongside other guidance, such as published exemplar materials or
 Guidance for Teaching.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                      1
                                                                                                               PMT


 GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE
 Positive Marking
 It should be remembered that learners are writing under examination conditions and credit should be given
 for what the learner writes, rather than adopting the approach of penalising him/her for any omissions. It
 should be possible for a very good response to achieve full marks and a very poor one to achieve zero
 marks. Marks should not be deducted for a less than perfect answer if it satisfies the criteria of the mark
 scheme, nor should marks be added as a consolation where they are not merited.
 For each question there is a list of indicative content which suggests the range of economic concepts,
 theory, issues and arguments which might be included in learners' answers. This is not intended to be
 exhaustive and learners do not have to include all the indicative content to reach the highest level of the
 mark scheme.
 The level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark to allocate to individual assessment objectives.
 These are shown in bands in the mark scheme. For each assessment objective a descriptor will indicate
 the different skills and qualities at the appropriate level. Learners' responses to questions are assessed
 against the relevant individual assessment objectives and they may achieve different bands within a single
 question. A mark will be awarded for each assessment objective targeted in the question and then totalled
 to give an overall mark for the question.

                                                                                                                             PMT


 GCE A LEVEL ECONOMICS - COMPONENT 2
 PREDICTED 2025 MARK SCHEME - ADVANCED


 Question 1

  1     1    Using the data, explain why UK productivity growth has slowed since 2008. [5]

 Band                   AO1                                  AO2                                    AO3

   2                                        2 marks                                 2 marks
                                            Good application                        Good analysis
                                            Strong use of the figures and/or text   Developed chain of reasoning
                                            to support the argument.                explaining the issue identified in the
                                                                                    question.

   1     1 mark                             1 mark                                  1 mark
         Knowledge                          Limited application                     Limited analysis
         Clear knowledge of the relevant    Use of the data is limited with few     Chain of reasoning is unconvincing
         concept is demonstrated.           direct references.                      or lacking in clarity.

   0     0 marks                            0 marks                                 0 marks
         No valid knowledge or              No valid application.                   No valid analysis.
         understanding.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 • Labour productivity: output per hour worked. Total factor productivity (TFP) is the Solow residual ·
 output growth unexplained by changes in labour and capital.
 AO2
 • Figure 1 shows the gap between actual productivity and the pre-2008 trend widening every year since
 the financial crisis.
 • Figure 2: UK business investment has been the lowest in the G7 · consistent with weak capital
 deepening.
 • Text: productivity growth in finance and ICT · the drivers of pre-crisis acceleration · has since slowed.
 • Text: growing sectors like hospitality and social care have inherently lower productivity than
 manufacturing.
 AO3
 • Weak business investment reduces the capital-labour ratio, which · from Y = f(K, L, A) · reduces
 output per worker for given technology and labour.
 • Structural shift of employment from manufacturing and financial services (high productivity) to hospitality
 and social care (low productivity) mechanically reduces aggregate productivity growth.
 • Slower innovation diffusion: the pre-crisis boom may have absorbed much of the one-off gains from ICT
 adoption; subsequent innovation has been slower to spread.
 • Labour market flexibility and abundant low-wage labour may have reduced the incentive to invest in
 labour-saving technology · the 'high-employment, low-productivity' UK equilibrium.
 • Balance-sheet recession following 2008: weak lending, zombie firms and debt overhang depressed
 investment and R&D for an extended period.
 • Allow any other valid chain of reasoning.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                         3
                                                                                                                               PMT


              Using the data, explain why an ageing population creates challenges for the
  1      2
              sustainability of the UK public finances. [5]

 Band                               AO2                                                           AO3

   3                                                                   3 marks
                                                                       Excellent analysis
                                                                       Well-developed chain of reasoning that examines
                                                                       several factors linked to the issue identified in the
                                                                       question.

   2      2 marks                                                      2 marks
          Good application                                             Good analysis
          Strong use of charts and/or text to support the points       Well-developed chain of reasoning explaining why the
          made.                                                        issue identified in the question holds.

   1      1 mark                                                       1 mark
          Limited application                                          Limited analysis
          Use of the data is limited with few direct references.       A chain of reasoning exists, but points are
                                                                       under-developed.

   0      0 marks                                                      0 marks
          No valid application.                                        No valid analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO2
 • Figure 3: OBR projects old-age dependency rising from 31% today to 47% by 2070.
 • Text: debt interest now absorbs around 8% of total spending.
 • Text: debt has risen from 35% of GDP in 2007 to nearly 100% today.
 AO3
 • An ageing population raises age-related spending: state pensions, NHS costs for older patients, and
 long-term care.
 • Simultaneously the share of the population of working age falls, reducing the tax base from which these
 costs must be financed.
 • Potential GDP growth slows because a smaller fraction of the population is in work; tax receipts grow
 more slowly than age-related spending.
 • If the resulting deficits are funded by additional debt, the debt-to-GDP ratio rises, raising interest
 payments and crowding out other spending.
 • Higher expected future deficits can raise risk premia on government borrowing, further increasing
 debt-service costs.
 • Allow any other valid chain of reasoning.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                   4
                                                                                                                          PMT


  1      3    Evaluate the effectiveness of supply-side policies in raising UK productivity. [10]

 Band                     AO2                                    AO3                                  AO4

   3      3 marks                                3 marks                              3-4 marks
          Excellent application                  Excellent analysis                   Excellent evaluation
          The data is used well on both sides    Well-developed and wider-ranging     Well-developed counter-arguments
          of the argument.                       chains of reasoning on one side of   and evaluative points. Answers at
                                                 the argument.                        the top of the band will have a
                                                                                      well-reasoned overall judgement.

   2      2 marks                                2 marks                              2 marks
          Good application                       Good analysis                        Good evaluation
          The data is well used on one side of   Developed chains of reasoning on     Developed counter-arguments or
          the argument.                          one side of the argument.            evaluative points.

   1      1 mark                                 1 mark                               1 mark
          Limited application                    Limited analysis                     Limited evaluation
          Use of the data is less effective.     Chains of reasoning exist but are    Counter-arguments are present but
          Relevant data has been used but not    under-developed.                     are under-developed.
          well developed.

   0      0 marks                                0 marks                              0 marks
          No valid application.                  No valid analysis.                   No valid evaluation.


 Indicative content
 AO2
 • Full expensing of business investment, R&D tax credits, planning reform and apprenticeship levy
 changes · all mentioned in the text.
 • Figure 2 shows UK investment has historically been weaker than G7 peers despite successive
 supply-side initiatives.
 • Post-pandemic rise in long-term sickness has pushed several hundred thousand people out of the labour
 force.
 AO3
 • Tax reliefs on business investment lower the user cost of capital, raising desired capital stock and
 thereby output per worker over time.
 • R&D tax credits raise the private return on innovation, which addresses the positive externality of
 knowledge spillovers.
 • Planning reform accelerates delivery of infrastructure, shifting aggregate supply right in the long run.
 • Apprenticeship and skills reform raises the quality of the labour force, directly raising productivity.
 • Deregulation can reduce frictions in labour and product markets, allowing resources to flow to their most
 productive uses.
 AO4
 • UK investment has remained below G7 peers despite decades of supply-side reform · evidence of
 limited effectiveness.
 • Supply-side policies face long time lags between announcement and effect, making them hard to
 evaluate and unpopular with politicians on short cycles.
 • Productivity also depends on factors outside government control: globalisation, demographics, secular
 stagnation, technology diffusion.
 • Some supply-side policies (e.g. tax cuts for corporations) can transfer resources to firms without
 delivering investment, reducing cost-effectiveness.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                             5
                                                                                                                   PMT


 • Policy reversals and uncertainty reduce the effectiveness of supply-side measures · businesses delay
 investment when the rules are in flux.
 • Education and skills interventions take a generation to affect the workforce · unlikely to shift productivity
 in the short run.
 • Macro context matters: supply-side policies work best when demand is strong. Weak aggregate demand
 leads to under-utilised capacity and reduced incentive to invest.
 • Overall judgement at the top of the band: supply-side policies are necessary but insufficient · they need
 to be consistently applied over long periods and complemented by macro stability to deliver measurable
 gains.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                       6
                                                                                                                          PMT


              Using the data, discuss the extent to which rising public debt limits the UK
  1      4
              government's ability to boost long-run economic growth. [10]

 Band                     AO2                                    AO3                                  AO4

   3      3 marks                                3 marks                              3-4 marks
          Excellent application                  Excellent analysis                   Excellent evaluation
          The data is used well on both sides    Well-developed and wider-ranging     Well-developed counter-arguments
          of the argument.                       chains of reasoning on one side of   and evaluative points. Answers at
                                                 the argument.                        the top of the band will have a
                                                                                      well-reasoned overall judgement.

   2      2 marks                                2 marks                              2 marks
          Good application                       Good analysis                        Good evaluation
          The data is well used on one side of   Developed chains of reasoning on     Developed counter-arguments or
          the argument.                          one side of the argument.            evaluative points.

   1      1 mark                                 1 mark                               1 mark
          Limited application                    Limited analysis                     Limited evaluation
          Use of the data is less effective.     Chains of reasoning exist but are    Counter-arguments are present but
          Relevant data has been used but not    under-developed.                     are under-developed.
          well developed.

   0      0 marks                                0 marks                              0 marks
          No valid application.                  No valid analysis.                   No valid evaluation.


 Indicative content
 AO2
 • Public sector net debt has risen from around 35% of GDP in 2007 to nearly 100% today.
 • Debt interest now absorbs 8% of total government spending · more than defence or transport.
 • Text: economists disagree about significance · some say fiscal space is genuinely constrained, others
 argue well-targeted investment is self-financing.
 • Figure 3 projects debt interest rising to over 6% of GDP by 2070 given ageing pressures.
 AO3
 • High debt levels raise debt-service costs, crowding out other productive spending.
 • Markets may require higher risk premia as debt rises, raising long-run borrowing costs.
 • Concerns about debt sustainability can reduce policy flexibility in future crises · less 'fiscal space' to
 respond to downturns.
 • High debt may constrain the government's ability to respond to adverse shocks (further pandemics,
 climate, geopolitical), raising long-run volatility.
 AO4
 • Well-targeted public investment (R&D, infrastructure, skills) can pay for itself over time by raising the
 growth rate · 'growth-friendly' fiscal expansion.
 • As long as the growth rate exceeds the interest rate (g > r), the debt-to-GDP ratio can fall even with
 ongoing deficits · the condition has held for much of the post-war period.
 • UK government debt is nearly all sterling-denominated and held by domestic institutions · currency
 crisis risk is low compared with emerging markets.
 • The Bank of England's QE programme purchased large quantities of gilts, reducing the net private-sector
 debt burden.
 • UK debt is still moderate by G7 standards · Japan's debt exceeds 250% of GDP without a crisis.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                             7
                                                                                                                  PMT


 • Ricardian equivalence and rational-expectations critiques suggest that private saving may offset
 government borrowing, reducing macroeconomic effect.
 • Policy choice matters: higher debt used to fund consumption is more constraining than higher debt used
 to fund investment with positive net returns.
 • Overall judgement at the top of the band: rising debt places a real but contingent constraint on policy · it
 limits the sustainable scale of further stimulus but does not prevent well-designed, investment-focused
 growth policy.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                       8
                                                                                                                         PMT


             Using the data, discuss whether the UK's productivity problems are primarily
  1     5
             structural or cyclical. [10]

 Band                    AO2                                    AO3                                  AO4

   3     3 marks                                3 marks                              3-4 marks
         Excellent application                  Excellent analysis                   Excellent evaluation
         The data is used well on both sides    Well-developed and wider-ranging     Well-developed counter-arguments
         of the argument.                       chains of reasoning on one side of   and evaluative points. Answers at
                                                the argument.                        the top of the band will have a
                                                                                     well-reasoned overall judgement.

   2     2 marks                                2 marks                              2 marks
         Good application                       Good analysis                        Good evaluation
         The data is well used on one side of   Developed chains of reasoning on     Developed counter-arguments or
         the argument.                          one side of the argument.            evaluative points.

   1     1 mark                                 1 mark                               1 mark
         Limited application                    Limited analysis                     Limited evaluation
         Use of the data is less effective.     Chains of reasoning exist but are    Counter-arguments are present but
         Relevant data has been used but not    under-developed.                     are under-developed.
         well developed.

   0     0 marks                                0 marks                              0 marks
         No valid application.                  No valid analysis.                   No valid evaluation.


 Indicative content
 AO2
 • Figure 1 shows a 15+ year gap between actual and pre-crisis trend · much too long to be purely
 cyclical.
 • Figure 2: UK investment has been structurally below G7 peers for decades.
 • Text: slowdown affects fast-growing sectors (finance, ICT) · a sectoral / structural story.
 • Text: mismeasurement of digital goods suggests some of the 'slowdown' may be statistical.
 AO3
 • The persistence of the slowdown over 15+ years (Figure 1) is strong evidence that the underlying causes
 are structural rather than purely cyclical.
 • Sectoral re-composition (finance/ICT slowing, social care/hospitality growing) is mechanically structural.
 • Persistently low investment (Figure 2) reduces the steady-state capital stock · a supply-side / structural
 channel.
 • Demographic ageing reduces the participation rate and potential growth · structural.
 AO4
 • Cyclical factors also matter: the 2008 recession followed by austerity, Brexit uncertainty, Covid and the
 energy shock have each damaged the capacity of the economy in ways that may partially reverse.
 • Hysteresis: cyclical shocks leave permanent scars via unemployment persistence, skill atrophy, and
 investment cancellation · making the cyclical/structural distinction porous.
 • The post-pandemic rise in long-term sickness could be interpreted either as cyclical (health shock) or
 structural (chronic condition increase).
 • Mismeasurement suggests productivity numbers themselves may overstate the slowdown · digital
 goods, free services and quality improvements are poorly captured.
 • The UK has underperformed other G7 economies on productivity even where they faced the same
 cyclical shocks, pointing to country-specific structural factors (planning, training, infrastructure).


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                            9
                                                                                                            PMT


 • Policy implications differ sharply: cyclical problems call for aggregate demand management; structural
 problems call for supply-side reform.
 • Overall judgement at the top of the band: the evidence points primarily to structural factors ·
 persistence, sector composition, weak investment · with cyclical shocks aggravating them. A durable
 recovery therefore needs supply-side reform alongside macro stability.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                     10
                                                                                                                          PMT


 Question 2

              Using the data, outline the characteristics of monopoly shown by dominant digital
  2     1
              platforms. [5]

 Band                              AO1                                                      AO2

   3                                                              3 marks
                                                                  Excellent application
                                                                  Good use of both examples / figures to illustrate the
                                                                  features of the market structure.

   2     2 marks                                                  2 marks
         Good knowledge                                           Good application
         Comprehensive understanding of the characteristics of    Good use of one of the examples to illustrate the
         the market structure.                                    features of the market structure.

   1     1 mark                                                   1 mark
         Limited knowledge                                        Limited application
         Understanding of the market structure is superficial,    Data references are superficial.
         incomplete or contains some inaccuracies.

   0     0 marks                                                  0 marks
         No valid knowledge or understanding.                     No valid application.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 • Monopoly: a market with a single dominant firm that has significant market power and is a price-maker,
 with high barriers to entry.
 • Characteristics: one/dominant seller, price-setter, barriers to entry, abnormal profits sustainable in the
 long run, price discrimination possible.
 AO2
 • Text: Big Tech firms account for well over half of global digital advertising and more than 80% of mobile
 OSs.
 • Figure 1: operating margins of 26-42% compared with S&P 500 average of 11% · abnormal profits on a
 very large scale.
 • Barriers: network effects, data advantage, economies of scale (MC near zero).
 • Text: CMA investigations and SMS designation powers demonstrate regulatory recognition of monopoly
 status.
 • Price-setting power: ability to charge high take rates on marketplaces or extract value from
 attention/data.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                             11
                                                                                                                           PMT


             Calculate TR, TC and abnormal profit at each output level for PlatformCo. Identify
  2     2
             profit-max and revenue-max outputs and explain why they differ. [7]

 Band                   AO1                                    AO2                                   AO3

   3                                          3 marks                                3 marks
                                              Excellent application                  Excellent analysis
                                              Use of the data is comprehensive       Well-developed chain of reasoning
                                              displaying all calculated values       explaining the output / employment
                                              correctly.                             at which the objective is achieved.

   2                                          2 marks                                2 marks
                                              Good application                       Good analysis
                                              Strong use of data with either the     Developed chain of reasoning
                                              revenue-side or the cost-side          showing how the optimal level of
                                              schedule calculated accurately. Or     output / employment is determined.
                                              calculated correctly in units rather   Some parts of the explanation are
                                              than per unit.                         incomplete or unclear.

   1     1 mark                               1 mark                                 1 mark
         Knowledge                            Limited application                    Limited analysis
         Knowledge of the relevant            Use of the data is limited with        Chain of reasoning is unconvincing
         profit-maximisation / employment /   significant inaccuracies in the        or lacking in clarity.
         output condition is shown.           calculations.

   0     0 marks                              0 marks                                0 marks
         No valid knowledge or                No valid application.                  No valid analysis.
         understanding.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 • Profit max: MR = MC (or equivalently the highest TR−TC).
 • Revenue max: MR = 0 (or the highest TR).
 AO2
 • TR (£m): 0, 90, 160, 210, 240, 250, 240, 210, 160.
 • TC (£m): 20, 35, 60, 95, 140, 195, 260, 335, 420.
 • Abnormal profit (£m): −20, 55, 100, 115, 100, 55, −20, −125, −260.
 • For TR correctly calculated: AO2: 1
 • For TC correctly calculated (or AP using correct TR): AO2: 1
 • For complete table with all three correctly: AO2: 3
 AO3
 • Profit-maximising output is Q = 3m users, where abnormal profit peaks at £115m.
 • Revenue-maximising output is Q = 5m users, where TR peaks at £250m.
 • They differ because revenue max ignores costs: as output rises beyond the profit-max level, the
 additional revenue from each extra unit (MR) is still positive but is now less than MC, so profit falls even as
 revenue continues to rise.
 • Mathematically: MR at profit-max equals MC (positive); MR at revenue-max equals zero. Between these
 two output levels MR is positive but less than MC.
 • A firm concerned purely with market share or with minimising regulatory attention may choose revenue
 max. A profit-maximising firm chooses profit max. A dominant tech platform concerned about losing share
 to new entrants may set output between the two.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                          12
                                      PMT


 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                 13
                                                                                                                                PMT


             Using a monopoly diagram, evaluate the welfare effects of breaking up a dominant
  2     3
             digital platform into smaller competing firms. [10]

 Band               AO1                           AO2                          AO3                            AO4

   3     3 marks                                                                                   3 marks
         Excellent knowledge                                                                       Excellent evaluation
         An accurate costs and                                                                     Impact on profits is fully
         revenue diagram is drawn                                                                  discussed with a
         showing both required                                                                     judgement as to whether
         shifts clearly.                                                                           profits rise or fall.

   2     2 marks                       2 marks                      2 marks                        2 marks
         Good knowledge                Good application             Good analysis                  Good evaluation
         An accurate costs and         The case is used to          Good chain of reasoning        The answer has developed
         revenue diagram is drawn      indicate how both changes    explaining the net effect on   counter-arguments or
         correctly showing one of      arise.                       profits.                       evaluative points.
         the two required shifts, or
         both with some errors.

   1     1 mark                        1 mark                       1 mark                         1 mark
         Limited knowledge             Limited application          Limited analysis               Limited evaluation
         A costs and revenue           The case is used to          Chain of reasoning is less     Counter-arguments are
         diagram is drawn but with     indicate how the change in   well-developed; link to        present but lack
         significant errors.           demand or costs arises,      profits is less clear.         development.
                                       but weakly.

   0     0 marks                       0 marks                      0 marks                        0 marks
         No valid knowledge or         No valid application.        No valid analysis.             No valid evaluation.
         understanding.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 • Clear monopoly diagram: D=AR, MR below AR, MC, AC; monopoly price Pm at Qm (MC=MR);
 competitive counterfactual price at Pc at Qc (where P=MC).
 • Deadweight welfare loss identified as the triangle between AR and MC from Qm to Qc.
 • Break-up lowers price towards Pc, expands output towards Qc, and reduces DWL.
 AO2
 • Text: Big Tech firms earn 26-42% margins vs 11% S&P average · large potential consumer surplus is
 captured as abnormal profit.
 • Text: network effects and economies of scale make natural monopoly a possibility.
 • Text: CMA's SMS regime is ex-ante and can partially achieve the same outcome without break-up.
 AO3
 • Break-up lowers the price and raises the quantity consumed, shifting producer surplus back to
 consumers and eliminating the deadweight loss.
 • Multiple competing firms will engage in greater non-price competition · faster product iteration, more
 choice, more privacy options.
 • Reduces the risk of leveraging dominance in adjacent markets (app store self-preferencing, search bias
 toward own products).
 • Restores dynamic competitive incentives · firms must innovate or lose share, rather than rely on
 monopoly rent.
 AO4


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                               14
                                                                                                              PMT


 • Where digital platforms exhibit strong network effects, a single platform is more valuable to users than
 several competing ones · break-up can destroy positive consumption externalities.
 • Economies of scale: if MC is near zero and fixed costs are high, smaller firms will have higher average
 costs and may not be able to offer free services.
 • R&D spending is concentrated in large firms (Figure 1 · Meta 29%, Alphabet 14%) and break-up could
 reduce aggregate R&D investment.
 • Enforcement complexity: defining the product boundaries of a digital platform is hard (the 'market' for
 search, social, operating systems is contested).
 • International dimension: unilateral UK action has little effect on globally-scaled platforms; EU/US
 alignment is needed.
 • Alternative remedies (interoperability rules, data portability, conduct requirements under the DMCC) may
 deliver much of the benefit without the costs of break-up.
 • Overall judgement at the top of the band: break-up can raise welfare where network effects are weak
 relative to market-power losses, but risks reducing welfare where platforms are genuinely increasing
 returns to scale. Less drastic remedies are likely to be more appropriate in most cases.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                      15
                                                                                                                            PMT


             Evaluate the view that sustained innovation requires firms to enjoy significant
  2     4
             monopoly power. [7]

 Band                    AO1                                      AO3                                   AO4

   3                                                                                    3 marks
                                                                                        Excellent evaluation
                                                                                        Strong evaluation which forms a
                                                                                        clear judgement on the statement.

   2     2 marks                                 2 marks                                2 marks
         Good knowledge                          Good analysis                          Good evaluation
         Clear understanding of the relevant     Clear chain of reasoning supporting    Developed counter-arguments or
         concept is shown.                       the view in the question.              evaluative points.

   1     1 mark                                  1 mark                                 1 mark
         Limited knowledge                       Limited analysis                       Limited evaluation
         Partial understanding of the relevant   The chain of reasoning lacks clarity   Counter-arguments are present but
         concept is shown.                       or detail and is unconvincing.         lack development.

   0     0 marks                                 0 marks                                0 marks
         No valid knowledge or                   No valid analysis.                     No valid evaluation.
         understanding.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 • Schumpeterian view: firms innovate in pursuit of monopoly rents; high expected post-innovation profits
 fund and justify R&D spending.
 • Alternative view: innovation is driven by competitive pressure · firms innovate to avoid being displaced.
 AO3
 • Monopoly profits fund the large fixed R&D costs associated with modern innovation (e.g. semiconductor
 foundries, AI training runs).
 • Patent protection grants temporary monopoly so that innovators can recoup sunk investment · without
 it, the free-rider problem reduces innovation below the social optimum.
 • Long-horizon investment requires stable and predictable returns; monopoly firms face lower risk premia
 on R&D.
 • Network effects and scale allow dominant firms to deploy new products to vast user bases faster and
 cheaper than a small firm could.
 AO4
 • The Arrow replacement effect suggests monopolists innovate less than competitive firms because new
 products cannibalise existing profits.
 • Empirical evidence (Aghion et al.) finds an 'inverted-U' relationship between competition and innovation
 · very high and very low competition both reduce R&D; moderate competition maximises it.
 • Historic periods of strong innovation (US antitrust era, 1940s-70s) saw large firms but also strong
 competitive constraints.
 • Start-ups and academic R&D deliver a disproportionate share of fundamental innovation, often without
 market power of their own.
 • Monopoly profits are often used for share buybacks and dividends rather than R&D · even Big Tech's
 14-29% R&D share shows most revenue goes elsewhere.
 • Technology diffusion slows when dominant firms can buy up potential disruptors ('killer acquisitions') ·
 monopoly power may therefore reduce long-run innovation.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                             16
                                                                                                      PMT


 • Overall judgement: some market power is necessary to reward innovation (hence IP rights), but
 unrestricted monopoly power is not · in fact, moderate competitive pressure seems to maximise R&D.
 Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                  17
                                                                                                                              PMT


             With reference to the data, discuss the extent to which ex-ante regulation (such as
  2     5    the DMCC Act 2024) can effectively constrain the market power of dominant digital
             platforms. [11]

 Band              AO1                           AO2                          AO3                          AO4

   3                                                               3 marks                      3-4 marks
                                                                   Excellent analysis           Excellent evaluation
                                                                   Well-developed               Very well-developed
                                                                   explanation with a clear     counter-arguments and
                                                                   chain of reasoning linking   evaluative points, with a
                                                                   directly to the question     well-reasoned overall
                                                                   focus.                       judgement at the top of the
                                                                                                band.

   2     2 marks                      2 marks                      2 marks                      2 marks
         Good knowledge               Good application             Good analysis                Good evaluation
         A clear knowledge of the     The answer is well           Developed explanation of     The answer has developed
         relevant concept is          contextualised, making use   the central chain of         counter-arguments or
         demonstrated.                of the case on both sides    reasoning but some           evaluative points.
                                      of the argument.             aspects are unclear.

   1     1 mark                       1 mark                       1 mark                       1 mark
         Limited knowledge            Limited application          Limited analysis             Limited evaluation
         Some knowledge of the        Some references to the       Candidate offers a limited   Counter-arguments are
         relevant concept is shown.   context, but these are not   or only partially correct    present, but none well
                                      well developed.              analysis.                    developed.

   0     0 marks                      0 marks                      0 marks                      0 marks
         No valid knowledge or        No valid application.        No valid analysis.           No valid evaluation.
         understanding.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 • Ex-ante regulation: rules imposed before harm occurs, based on firms' market position, rather than
 waiting for case-by-case antitrust litigation (ex-post).
 • The DMCC Act 2024 gives the CMA powers to designate firms with 'strategic market status' (SMS) and
 impose bespoke conduct requirements.
 AO2
 • Text: EU DMA (2022) takes a similar ex-ante approach; US has relied on ex-post litigation.
 • Text: 'each framework faces difficult trade-offs between deterring harmful conduct, preserving incentives
 to innovate, and keeping up with fast-moving technological change'.
 • Figure 1: very large operating margins and R&D intensity suggest significant abnormal profit available to
 fund compliance · but also significant stakes of disruption.
 AO3
 • Ex-ante regulation is faster than ex-post enforcement, which can take 5-10 years per case · digital
 markets move far faster than the courts.
 • Bespoke conduct requirements can address specific harms (e.g. app store self-preferencing,
 interoperability gaps) without the complexity of structural remedies.
 • Certainty for market participants: new entrants know what behaviours are prohibited and can plan
 accordingly, lowering entry barriers.
 • Continuous engagement between regulator and firm allows adjustments as markets evolve · more
 flexible than litigation.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                              18
                                                                                                                PMT


 AO4
 • Regulatory capture: dominant firms have far more resources to engage with regulators than new
 entrants, leading to outcomes that protect incumbents.
 • Technology moves faster than regulation · by the time a conduct rule is in force, the relevant market
 may have shifted (search to AI, messaging to video).
 • Unilateral UK action affects only a small market (~4% of global digital ad spend); firms can restructure
 products or corporate arrangements to avoid UK-specific rules.
 • Compliance costs raise entry barriers for smaller rivals who must now anticipate regulatory scrutiny if
 they succeed · perversely reinforcing dominance.
 • Chilling effect on innovation: firms fear that successful products will trigger regulatory intervention,
 reducing incentive to invest.
 • International coordination matters: the UK, EU and US moving in broadly the same direction increases
 effectiveness; divergence creates jurisdictional arbitrage.
 • Enforcement requires significant technical capacity within the CMA · budget and skills may be a binding
 constraint.
 • Alternative remedies (structural break-up, mandatory data portability, tax on digital services) have their
 own trade-offs and are not always available.
 • Overall judgement at the top of the band: ex-ante regulation of SMS firms is likely to be more effective
 than pure ex-post antitrust in fast-moving digital markets, but effectiveness depends heavily on
 international coordination, regulatory resource, and avoiding regulatory capture. It is a necessary but
 insufficient tool.
 Allow any other valid points.
